text: Surface Reconstruction and Adsorbate-Induced Structure Evolution at Copper Electrodes during CO2 Reduction The surface reconstruction of Copper (Cu) during the catalytic CO2 reduction reaction ($CO_2$RR) has been widely studied, attributing the unique performance of Cu catalysts towards $CO_2$RR. The activation process associated with the surface roughening and related Cu catalytic activity is attributed to atomic vacancies and adatoms due to Cu surface reconstruction. However, the atomistic structure of the reconstructed surface and the related Cu active sites that exist at the catalyst surface under different adsorbate coverages remains unclear. Here, the chemical space of Cu surface reconstruction at mixed CO and H adsorbates was investigated using grand canonical density functional theory (GCDFT) and global optimization methods. A grand canonical ensemble of Cu(100), Cu(111), and Cu(711) interfaces was constructed from thousands of unique states. These simulations demonstrated that there is a considerable vertical displacement of surface Cu atoms in the mixed CO and H coverage regime. This regime is kinetically controlled, and thus quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo (QKC) simulations were performed to understand the non-equilibrium structural evolution of the electrode during a cathodic scan. These simulations revealed that the Cu surface gets trapped in metastable states containing Cu-CO species. The adsorption of these Cu-CO species is due to the cooperative effects of the atop CO adsorbates and the surrounding H adsorbates that “lubricate” the Cu surface by increasing the Cu-Cu bond-breaking propensity. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and free energy simulations revealed a wave-like diffusion of Cu-CO species, which leads to the dimerization of Cu-CO species and further catalyzing the $CO_2$RR reaction. In summary, this study provides new insights into the atomistic structure evolution of Cu catalysts during $CO_2$RR and highlights the simulation strategy that can be applied to investigate the non-equilibrium interfaces and structures of other complex transition metal electrodes in the context of heterogeneous electrocatalysis.
